Title: Antimenes Hydria
Description of Object(s):
The pot stands 46 cm high (18 1/8 in.) and is crafted in ceramic using the Black-Figure technique. The main panel depicts four barbarian horsemen riding to the right—three on black horses and one on a white. Dressed in pointed caps, long-sleeved tunics, and patterned trousers, the riders are likely Scythians, identified by their distinctive attire.

Beneath the main scene, a frieze of animals unfolds, centered around a dramatic group of two lions attacking a prey. On the shoulder of the vessel, a departure scene shows a warrior mounting a chariot, observed by five other figures, possibly family members or companions.
Object Type: Vessel – pots, amphorae, vases, kraters

Title: Weary Herakles (Top Half)
Description of Object(s):
This Roman sculpture was modeled after an original 4th century B.C. bronze statue of Weary Herakles by the Greek master Lysippos of Sikyon. The version is identified as the "Herakles Farnese" type of sculpture on the basis of its similarity to a more complete copy in Naples. shows an aged Herakles holding the Apples of the Hesperides in his right hand, behind his back, and leaning on a club covered with the skin of the Nemean lion and a cloak, which he has placed under his left armpit. Herakles' hair is disheveled, his brow is knotted, and his eyes are sunken. The "Herakles Farnese" type was very popular in antiquity; many replicas and variants have been identified. This particular example was probably carved in the Hadrianic or Antonine period.

The statue is incomplete. Both arms have been broken off, the left at the elbow, the right higher up. The lower part of this statue, now in the Antalya Museum, was excavated in the South Baths at Perge.
Object Type: Sculpture – statues, carvings, bronzes, reliefs, figurines
Findspot: Perge, Turkiye. Marble possibly from Paros or Aphrodisias

Title: Cornell Cuneiform Tablets
Description of Object(s):
The 10,000 inscribed clay blocks date from the 4th millenium BC include the Garsana archive, the private records of a 21st century BC Sumerian princess in the city of Garsana that has made scholars rethink the role of women in the ancient kingdom of Ur. The administrative records show Simat-Ishtaran ruled the estate after her husband died. 

Other tablets provide detailed administrative records of ancient life, including the procedures for temple rituals, the resettlement of refugees and the output of agricultural lands.
Object Type: Information Artifact
Museum Name: Herbert F. Johnson Museum of Art - Cornell University

Blessed is he who sees [it] and pronounces a blessing"

Title: Gold Phiale
Description of Object(s): The phiale is a 24-karat gold libation bowl, dated to the 4th century BCE. The platter, known as a 'phiale mesomphalos', was used by the ancient Greeks to honor their gods. The bowl would have begun as a two-pound solid piece of gold, before it was hammered by a master artisan into a shallow platter. After, shapes of acorns, beechnuts and bees were indented into the outer workings of the bowl. In the middle, a round knob symbolized the mythological navel of the universe. An inscription was also inscribed along the edge, written in a Greek-Doric dialect that was spoken in Sicilian-Greek colonies.
Object Type: Vessel – pots, amphorae, vases, kraters
Dealer: Vincenzo Cammarata | William Veres | Robert Haber

Title: Michael Steinhardt Collection
Receiving Country: Italy | Greece | Türkiye | Lebanon | Bulgaria | Egypt | Syria | Libya
Country of Origin: Italy | Lebanon | Greece | Türkiye | Syria | Israel | Jordan | Egypt | Bulgaria
Description of Object(s):
Among the highlights of the seized objects: 

A rare Greek kouros, purchased on November 17, 2000 directly from the antiquities trafficker Robert Hecht for USD $2,348,500. Hecht included pictures of the sculpture soon after it was looted.

A situla, which surfaced on the art market in October 1995, when Robin Symes sold it to the Beierwaltes for USD $320,000. In 2006, the Beierwaltes consigned it to Phoenix Ancient Art. Steinhardt purchased it in 2009 from Phoenix Ancient Art for USD $250,000. In Symes' archives, prosecutors found photos of the same situla taken in 1995 by the Basel-based Dieter Widmer. It had no handle and was still covered in dirt and encrustations, signs of recent looting. Today valued at USD $320,000, the looted situla will be returned to Italy.

An Assyrian Gold Bowl that Svyatoslav (aka Slav) Konkin, a Russian antiquities dealer, hand carried on a flight from Hong Kong to Newark NJ. He was detained by authorities but allowed to deliver the bowl to Steinhardt, who purchased it in July 2000 for USD $150,000. Prosecutors say the bowl had been looted in Nimrud, Iraq, where similar examples have been found. Similar gold objects were stolen from the Baghdad Museum, while others were looted and sold by ISIS. It will be returned to Iraq.

A Veiled Head of a Woman that surfaced on the art market on November 10, 2000, when Steinhardt purchased it from Michael Ward for USD $1,200,000. Ward noted it was "possibly from North Africa,"   had "earthy deposit" and a "clean break" on the neck. Prosecutors say the bust was looted from a tomb in the ancient city of Cyrene, in modern day Libya. Excavations there shut down during unrest in 1999, and looters began sacking the city's ancient tombs.

A Togate Figure that Phoenix Ancient Art sold to Steinhardt for USD $800,000 in June 1993, stating it was from "Perusia [sic], Umbria, Italy." Steinhardt later consigned it back to Phoenix for sale. Polaroids found in the archives of Giacomo Medici show the togate with a companion figure, both broken and dirt-encrusted sitting atop an Italian newspaper. The Etruscan inscription along the base suggests it was indeed likely found near Perugia.

An Orpheus Mosaic that first surfaced on the art market in 1991, when Robert Haber shipped it from Geneva to the United States for Steinhardt. Steinhardt paid Haber USD $440,000 for the mosaic in Oct 1991 and had it sent to California for restoration. The restorers noted it had been crudely "cut into five sections in order to simplify lifting it from its original site," a Roman villa in Sicily. A polaroid of the mosaic sent to Haber by Geneva shipper Mat Securitas shows it before restoration.

A Griffin Cauldron with Stand that Steinhardt purchased in April 2003 from Robert Hecht with no prior provenance for USD $475,000. Other records suggest he paid USD $3,000,000 for the completed piece, which had surfaced in fragments. Photos recovered from Hecht show the cauldron soon after it emerged from a tomb in Lazio, dirt encrusted and broken in two.

The Ercolano Fresco, showing Hercules as an infant strangling a snake. This fresco was looted from Herculaneum in 1995, and Steinhardt purchased the fresco for $650,000. The fresco is currently valued at $1 million.

(It looks like 51 of these items went back to Italy, remaining are from other countries: https://www.documentcloud.org/documents/24244920-2023-06-27-15-51-2021-12-13-to-steinhardt-178p-o/)

Title: Don Miller Collection
Year Returned: 2016 (MHA Nation, North Dakota, United States) | 2018 (Colombia) | 2018 (Canada) | 2019 (China) | 2019 (Mexico) | 2020 (Haiti) | 2020 (Papua New Guinea) | 2022 (Peru) | 2022 (Pokagon Band Potawatomi, Indiana and Michigan, United States) | 2022 (Olgala Sioux Nation, South Dakota, United States) | 2022 (Dominican Republic)
Receiving Country: Haiti | Papua New Guinea | Mexico | China | Indigenous or sovereign nation/tribe
Description of Object(s):
The collection contained about 42,000 items, including Native American artifacts, human remains, and objects from South America, Asia, the Caribbean, and the Pacific. Seized material included roughly 7,000 items, specific examples include 2,500-year-old Chinese jewelry, a dugout canoe from the Amazon, dinosaur eggs, a fossilized crocodile skull, Ming Dynasty vases, shrunken heads, Aztec figurines, and Celtic axes.

A significant portion of the collection consisted of human remains taken from Indigenous burial sites across the United States and Mexico. Thousands of bones were recovered, but osteological analysis revealed that Miller had often combined pieces from different individuals (e.g., skulls, mandibles, and teeth) into composite displays described as “Frankenstein-like” that he would show to guests invited to his private collection. This staging inflated earlier estimates of 500 skeletons; later assessment suggested a more accurate total of about 168 individuals.
Object Type: Various

Title: Stanley Museum Benin Bronzes
Description of Object(s):
Wooden Hen Figure: dimensions 19 x 8 x 11 in. (48.26 x 20.32 x 27.94 cm). Description per Christopher Roy: Hen carved from one piece of wood; dark brown surface coloration. There is an iron nail at the outer side of the PL foot. Inscribed on the reverse: “Benin. WBSTR:1899”. Bottom is inscribed: Benin. W. Africa. WBSTR 1899. CMS316 is applied in red ink on both the side and the bottom…The post which projects from the back of the hen once supported a small elephant's tusk (Fagg 1968: no.150). The geometric patterns carved into the surface of the figure represent the bird's feathers. "The design on the base is a kind of trademark of the carver's guild ("igbesanmwan") and is called "oba n'iri agbon", the pattern of the rope of the world. It symbolizes infinity/endless wisdom of the ancestors" (Ben-Amos 1978). The significance of the forms which sweep downward from the post, on either side of the body, has been the subject of some speculation (Pitt-Rivers 1900:84), although they appear to represent the bird's wings held in the mating gesture with tips brushing the ground… The hen's crest has been broken off, probably at the time the piece was removed from Benin. 

Bronze Relief Plaque: Dimensions 11 1/2 x 9 3/4 x 3/4 in. (29.21 x 24.77 x 1.91 cm). The brass plaque (called ama in Edo language) depicts a high-ranking officer from the Benin Court. Shown in relief before the schematic rendering of flora, he wears a woven cap, a beaded sash over the shoulder, a leopard-face pendant on the hip and a ceremonial sword (eben) in one hand. Condition reports indicate significant damage to the plaque, including a large, approximately 2.5-inch hole between the sword and the bicep along the forearm of the figure and a crack from the armpit to the bottom of the thigh on the right side.
Object Type: Sculpture – statues, carvings, bronzes, reliefs, figurines | Religious Work – crucifixes, shrine objects, icons, religious texts | Fragment
Findspot: Benin Royal Palace of Oba Ovonranwmen
Current Location: Royal Palace of the Oba of Benin
Museum Name: University of Iowa Stanley Museum of Art

Title: Head of a King (Oba)
Description of Object(s):
A bronze head (dimensions 26.7 x 19.7 x 21.6 cm)  representing an oba, or king, of the Edo people of Benin, West Africa.

Per Ugochukwu-Smooth C. Nzewi: Cast in bronze, the crown mirrors the coral-bead headdress worn by the oba (king). The actual beaded crown consists of tiny red beads stitched together with brown vegetable fiber. The beads carry the essence of the office of oba. A single cowrie shell sits on the forehead, flanked by three scarification patterns called ikharo above each amplified eye. The tubular bead-collar covers the neck and chin, extending all the way to the lower lip. With its remarkably stylized features, the crowned head is a portrait of elegant symmetry and dignified comportment. The absence of a flange at the base suggests that it is an eighteenth-century-style commemorative head.
Object Type: Sculpture – statues, carvings, bronzes, reliefs, figurines | Religious Work – crucifixes, shrine objects, icons, religious texts
Findspot: Benin Royal Palace of Oba Ovonranwmen
